title: Floor Channels
desc: The carved grooves in a dormant work chamber need cleaning before the next Conjuror session starts pretending dust is part of the method.

steps.text:
The work chamber floor looks decorative until you stand in it long enough to notice how nothing carved there is accidental. Concentric channels run through the stone. Marker sockets line the outer ring. Fine pale residue sits in the grooves where the last session left more behind than the chamber attendant likes to see. You are not asked to understand the work done here. You are asked to keep the room from looking as though understanding it would be impossible.

The chamber attendant kneels beside the outer ring with a brush and a tray of ceramic markers. "Brush the carved channels clean if you want the floor itself," she says. "Or seat the boundary markers back into the sockets if you prefer the perimeter. Both need doing before the Conjurors begin insisting the room was ready when they found it."

options.label: Brush the carved channels clean of pale residue
Seat the ceramic boundary markers around the ring

steps.text:
The residue is fine as flour and determined to stay in the grooves where the last session packed it. You follow the channels one arc at a time, lifting the powder free with a narrow brush and collecting it in a covered tray before it can drift back across the floor. Once the lines are clear, the carving regains its sharpness. What had been a dusty pattern becomes deliberate stone again, each groove catching the chamber light with crisp edges.

The attendant runs two fingers above one cleaned line without touching it and seems relieved by the result. "Good. A room like this should feel intentional even to people who do not belong in it." That includes you, presumably, but she says it without malice.

options.label: Cap the residue tray and leave the channels cleanly cut

steps.text:
The boundary markers are glazed ceramic wedges, each one numbered on the underside and shaped to fit one socket only. Two are reversed, one is sitting proud of the floor, and another has somehow migrated three positions down the ring as though the chamber tidied itself badly overnight. You match each marker to its proper notch, seat it flush in the stone, and work around the circle until the perimeter stops looking improvised.

When the last wedge clicks into place, the whole chamber changes in a way that is hard to explain and easy to feel. Not magical exactly. More like balanced. The attendant makes a final circuit around the outer ring, then nods with clear satisfaction. "Better. Now the room may resume doing unsettling work on a properly arranged foundation."

options.label: Stack the spare wedges and leave the perimeter true
